quiz.title: Unit 8 Quiz 9, Grade 10 Biology
quiz.subtitle: Disease Transmission: Routes, Vectors, and Prevention
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Fecal-Oral Transmission, STIs, Droplet vs Airborne, Zoonotic Diseases, Vector-Borne Diseases, HIV/Hepatitis Transmission, Waterborne Diseases, Disease Prevention

questions.question: Fecal-oral transmission involves:
questions.explanation: Fecal-oral transmission occurs when pathogens from feces contaminate food or water that is then consumed. Examples include cholera, typhoid, and hepatitis A. Airborne transmission involves aerosols, vector bites involve insects, and skin contact is direct contact.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Which of the following is a sexually transmitted infection (STI)?
questions.explanation: HIV, Hepatitis B, Syphilis, and Gonorrhea are sexually transmitted infections. Conjunctivitis (pink eye) is usually caused by bacteria or viruses but is not primarily classified as an STI, though some forms can be transmitted sexually.

questions.question: Diseases that spread through droplets include:
questions.explanation: Influenza and COVID-19 spread through respiratory droplets from coughing and sneezing. Malaria and dengue are vector-borne, cholera and typhoid are fecal-oral, and hookworm/schistosoma are soil/water-borne helminths.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Which is an example of zoonotic disease?
questions.explanation: Zoonotic diseases are transmitted from animals to humans. Rabies (dogs/bats), Ebola (bats/primates), plague (rodents/fleas), and influenza (birds/pigs) are zoonotic. Conjunctivitis is typically not zoonotic.

questions.question: Direct transmission does NOT include:
questions.explanation: Sneezing into air is droplet transmission, not direct contact. Kissing, handshakes, and sexual intercourse involve direct physical contact between individuals.

questions.question: Plague (Yersinia pestis) is transmitted by:
questions.explanation: Plague is transmitted by fleas that infest rats and other rodents. Ticks transmit Lyme disease, mosquitoes transmit malaria/dengue, and lice transmit typhus.

questions.question: Ebola virus spreads primarily through:
questions.explanation: Ebola spreads through direct contact with blood, bodily fluids, or tissues of infected individuals or animals. It is not airborne, not mosquito-borne, and not primarily food-borne.

questions.question: Cholera is transmitted via:
questions.explanation: Cholera is transmitted through the fecal-oral route via contaminated water or food. It is not vector-borne, not primarily sexually transmitted, and not airborne.

questions.question: Select all that apply: HIV is transmitted through:
questions.explanation: HIV is transmitted through blood, sexual fluids, breast milk, and from mother to child during pregnancy/childbirth. It is NOT transmitted by mosquito bites, casual contact, or air.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Hepatitis B transmission occurs via:
questions.explanation: Hepatitis B is transmitted through blood (transfusions, needles), sexual contact, and from mother to child during birth. It is NOT transmitted by handshakes, hugging, or casual contact.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Waterborne diseases include:
questions.explanation: Cholera, typhoid, and hepatitis A are waterborne diseases transmitted through contaminated water. Hepatitis B is blood-borne/sexually transmitted, not waterborne.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Diseases like chickenpox spread through:
questions.explanation: Chickenpox spreads through direct contact with blisters and airborne droplets from coughing/sneezing. It is not vector-borne and not transmitted through contaminated food.

questions.question: Vehicle transmission includes spread via:
questions.explanation: Vehicle transmission refers to the spread of pathogens through contaminated inanimate materials (vehicles) such as water, food, air, or medical equipment. Contaminated water is a classic example of vehicle transmission. Sexual contact, kissing, and handshaking are forms of direct contact transmission.

questions.question: Which of the following disease-prevention strategies involves closing water systems and clearing stagnant water?
questions.explanation: Closing water systems and clearing stagnant water targets mosquito breeding sites, primarily for dengue prevention (Aedes mosquitoes). Influenza prevention involves vaccines and hygiene, HIV prevention involves safe sex practices, and cholera prevention involves clean water/sanitation.

questions.question: Which disease is primarily prevented by using insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor spraying, and removing breeding sites?
questions.explanation: Malaria prevention focuses on controlling mosquito vectors (Anopheles) through bed nets, indoor spraying, and eliminating breeding sites. Influenza prevention uses vaccines, tuberculosis prevention uses BCG vaccine and isolation, cholera prevention uses clean water.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered disease transmission routes and prevention! 🦠
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the differences between direct, droplet, and airborne transmission.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on STIs, zoonotic diseases, and vector-borne illnesses.
scoreMessages.0: Please review fecal-oral transmission, HIV/Hepatitis routes, and malaria prevention.
